old_claim.note: Anatomical Illustration provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Hippocratic case histories established the written individual-case tradition that early modern practitioners extended into serial practice casebooks.

source_shape.source_locator: The review begins its history of case reports with individual case histories in the Hippocratic Epidemics, probably written around 400 BC.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Hippocratic written case histories are the documented early lineage for later medical case-report and casebook traditions.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This replacement follows the same documentary form across periods; anatomical illustration is a different publication practice and is not needed to record consultations.

ontology_before: Medical Case Records depended on Renaissance anatomical illustration, conflating images of anatomy with serial records of individual patients and consultations.
ontology_after: Early Modern Medical Casebooks inherit from the written individual-case tradition represented by Hippocratic Case Histories.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: medical_case_records now depends on medical_case_notes and no longer directly depends on anatomical_illustration.
would_reject_if: A direct source shows that the Forman-Napier casebook practice required anatomical atlases or illustration as a record-making capability.
The medical_case_notes node is rescoped away from written individual patient histories.
short_reason: Written Hippocratic cases provide the documentary lineage; anatomical illustration is a separate artifact class.
